questions.question: PixelRNN and DALL-E 1 both used autoregressive generation for images but scaled worse than GPT-on-text. What was the core generation-order problem they shared?
options: They required per-pixel positional embeddings that grew prohibitively large at high resolution
Their VQ codebooks were too small to capture image diversity, forcing repeated sampling
Visiting pixels or tokens in raster (1D) order forces early tokens to commit without any global image context, breaking the predictable scaling relationship seen in language models
They used recurrent architectures that prevented parallelism during training, throttling compute scaling
questions.explanation: In raster order, the top-left corner token is generated with no knowledge of what the full image will look like. This 'early commitment' breaks the coarse-to-fine inductive bias that image statistics have. VAR fixes this by predicting an entire scale at once, conditioning each scale on all prior (coarser) scales — the model always has global context before generating fine details.

questions.question: VAR uses a multi-scale residual VQ tokenizer that produces K token grids z_1, z_2, ..., z_K. The decoder reconstructs the image as sum(upsample(embed(z_k))) over all k. Why is this called 'residual' VQ?
options: Each scale uses an independent codebook trained to minimise the reconstruction residual of the previous scale's codebook
Each scale's tokens encode what the coarser scales missed — scale k captures the residual left by scales 1..k-1 — so the decoder reconstructs by summing all scale embeddings
Residual connections bypass certain scale levels to prevent codebook collapse at small codebook sizes
The tokenizer is a residual neural network that adds Gaussian noise at each scale for regularisation
questions.explanation: The multi-scale VQ tokenizer is trained so that summing the upsampled embeddings across all scales reconstructs the original latent. Scale 1 (1×1) captures the coarsest summary; each subsequent scale z_k captures what scales 1..k-1 left unrepresented. This residual decomposition means the generative model only has to predict the incremental residual at each step, not the full image from scratch.

questions.question: Within a single scale k, VAR generates all tokens in one transformer forward pass — parallel, not sequential. Why is this valid when standard GPT-style AR cannot generate tokens in parallel?
options: VAR uses bidirectional attention within each scale, trading causality for speed at the cost of quality
All scale-k tokens share the same positional embedding so their logits are identical and can be sampled once
VAR uses a separate non-autoregressive decoder per scale, trained independently with masked language modelling
All tokens at scale k condition on the same prior-scale context (scales 1..k-1) and have no sequential dependency on each other within the scale
questions.explanation: The autoregressive dependency in VAR is across scales (scale k depends on scales 1..k-1), not within a scale. Every token at scale k conditions on exactly the same set of prior-scale tokens, so they can be sampled simultaneously in one forward pass. This reduces K-scale generation to K transformer calls instead of K × (tokens per scale) sequential calls.

questions.question: The Tian et al. 2024 VAR paper claimed a property for image generation that no prior image AR model had demonstrated. What was it, and why did the paper treat it as significant?
options: VAR achieved zero-shot text-to-image generation without CLIP supervision, surprising the community
VAR's FID on ImageNet follows a power-law scaling curve with compute — the same predictable relationship GPT has with perplexity — making generation quality forecastable from compute
VAR trained 10× faster than DiT at matched parameter counts due to the multi-scale tokeniser's compressed token sequence
VAR's per-scale parallelism allowed it to use diffusion-style noise schedules within each scale, combining two paradigms
questions.explanation: The power-law scaling result was VAR's headline claim: FID ∝ compute^(-exponent), just like language models. This is significant because it means you can predict how much better the model gets by spending more compute — no guessing. Prior image AR models (PixelCNN, VQVAE-based) did not exhibit this clean scaling, so VAR was the first image generation approach to match language models on predictable scaling behaviour.

questions.question: The Build code implements a scale-structured attention mask where tokens at scale k attend to all tokens at scales 1..k but not k+1 onward. Which standard causal masking property does this mirror, and what intra-scale pattern does it enforce?
options: It mirrors decoder-only causal masking; within scale k tokens attend to each other autoregressively in row-major order
It mirrors encoder-only bidirectional attention; within scale k tokens attend to all prior scales plus each other bidirectionally
It mirrors decoder-only causal masking across scales; within a scale all tokens share the same prior-scale context so the mask is fully dense — no intra-scale causality
It mirrors prefix language modelling; scale 1 is the prefix (attended to fully) while later scales are the completion (causal within each scale)
questions.explanation: Scale order is causal: scale k can see scales 1..k-1 but not k+1..K. This is the same pattern as token order in a causal LM. Within a scale, however, all tokens attend to the same prior-scale context and have no ordering constraint — the mask is fully dense intra-scale, enabling one-shot parallel generation per scale. The code's attention mask encodes this by treating all scale-k positions as if they arrived simultaneously.

questions.question: The doc compares VAR to diffusion (DDPM/DiT) on two axes: number of generation passes and what each pass produces. For a 256×256 image, VAR uses K=10 scale passes and DiT uses 28–50 denoising steps. What does VAR produce per pass that DiT cannot, and what inference-speed conclusion does the paper draw?
options: DiT produces a full-resolution image at each step but at lower quality; VAR produces full resolution immediately but must refine over K steps — they are equivalent in speed

VAR produces an entire spatial scale (all tokens at resolution 2^k × 2^k) in one pass in parallel; DiT produces a small noise increment across the full image — VAR needs fewer passes and each pass is parallel within scale, making inference faster
Both methods produce the same number of total tokens per pass; VAR is faster only because its transformer is smaller than DiT
VAR produces higher-resolution tokens per pass than DiT, but DiT compensates by using fewer total steps, so they have equal FID at the same wall-clock time
questions.explanation: Each VAR pass generates an entire scale's token grid in parallel — at scale k=5 it outputs 4×4=16 tokens simultaneously. Each DiT denoising step processes the full spatial resolution but makes only a small noise correction. VAR uses ~10 passes vs DiT's 28–50, and each VAR pass is parallel within scale. The paper reports that VAR matches or beats DiT on class-conditional ImageNet at the same compute budget.
